coredata.dc:title: Pentaethylenehexamine and d-penicillamine co-functionalized graphene quantum dots for fluorescent detection of mercury(II) and glutathione and bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Ping, Yan
Ruiyi, Li
Yongqiang, Yang
Zaijun, Li
Zhiguo, Gu
Guangli, Wang
Junkang, Liu
coredata.dc:description: Pentaethylenehexamine and d-penicillamine co-functionalized graphene quantum dots (PEHA-GQD-DPA) was made via one two-step thermal pyrolysis. The resulting PEHA-GQD-DPA is composed of the graphene sheets with an average size of 3.16 nm and the rich of functional groups. It gives an ultra strong fluorescence emission with the fluorescent quantum yield of 90.91% and sensitive and selective optical response towards Hg2+. The fluorescence intensity linearly decreases with the increase of Hg2+ in the range of 1.0 × 10−10–2 × 10−4 M with the detection limit of 4.6 × 10−11 M (S/N = 3). No species tested interfere with detection of Hg2+. The fluorescence quenched by Hg2+ can be well recovered by glutathione. The fluorescence intensity linearly increases with the increase of glutathione in the range of 5 × 10−8–2.5 × 10−6 M with the detection limit of 1.7 × 10−8 M (S/N = 3). The PEHA-GQD-DPA as a fluorescence probe has been successfully applied in determination of Hg2+ in natural water and glutathione in human serum and SW480 cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence probe
Sequential recognition
Hg2+
Glutathione
SW480 cell

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sequential Recognition of Hg2+ and Glutathione in Living Cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115 ), Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision Inspection Institute (KJ(Y) 2017014 ) and MOE & SAFEA for the 111 Project ( B13025 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The procedure for synthesis of PEHA-GQD-DPA.
TEM image (A) (Insert: particle size distribution), IR spectrum (B) and AFM images of PEHA-GQD-DPA (C and D).
Absorption spectrum (a) and fluorescence emission spectrum (b) of PEHA-GQD-DPA. Inset: optical photographs of the PEHA-GQD-DPA solution under white light (c) and ultraviolet light irradiation (d).

A: Fluorescence spectra of the PEHA-GQD-DPA in the presence of 1 × 10−10, 2 × 10−10, 4 × 10−10, 1 × 10−9, 2 × 10−9, 4 × 10−9, 1 × 10−8, 2 × 10−8, 4 × 10−8, 1 × 10−7, 2 × 10−7, 4 × 10−7, 1 × 10−6, 2 × 10−6, 4 × 10−6, 1 × 10−5, 2 × 10−5, 4 × 10−5, 1 × 10−4 and 2 × 10−4 M Hg2+ (from top to bottom). B: The relationship curve of peak fluorescence intensity with the logarithm of Hg2+ concentration.

Fluorescence spectra of the Hg-PEHA-GQD-DPA in the presence of 2.6 × 10−6, 2.2 × 10−6, 2.0 × 10−6, 1.7 × 10−6, 1.5 × 10−6, 1.2 × 10−6, 1 × 10−6, 9 × 10−7, 7 × 10−7, 5 × 10−7, 2.5 × 10−7, 5 × 10−8 and 0.0 M of glutathione (from top to bottom). Insert: the relationship curve of peak fluorescence intensity with glutathione concentration.

Images of SW480 cells: (a) bright field image of cells incubated with PEHA-GQD-DPA, (b) fluorescence image of (a), (c) fluorescence image of (b) in the presence of 2 × 10−5 M Hg2+ and (d) fluorescence image of (c) in the presence of glutathione.
Determination results of Hg2+ obtained from the proposed method in water samples (N = 5).
Determination results of glutathione in serum samples obtained from the proposed and HPLC methods (N = 5).

row.entry: Detected glutathione obtained by the proposed method (μM)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
Results expressed as: X ± st
n
(n = 5), where X is the mean of n observations of x, s is the standard deviation, t is distribution value chosen for the desired confidence level, the t- and F-values refer to comparison of the proposed method with the hexokinase method. Theoretical value at 95% confidence limits: F = 6.39, t = 2.78.

head.ce:title: Pentaethylenehexamine and d-penicillamine co-functionalized graphene quantum dots for fluorescent detection of mercury(II) and glutathione and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Graphene Products Quality Supervision and Inspection Center (Jiangsu), Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision and Inspection Institute Branch of Wuxi, 214174, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Graphene Products Quality Supervision and Inspection Center (Jiangsu)
Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision and Inspection Institute Branch of Wuxi

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pentaethylenehexamine and d-penicillamine co-functionalized graphene quantum dots (PEHA-GQD-DPA) was made via one two-step thermal pyrolysis. The resulting PEHA-GQD-DPA is composed of the graphene sheets with an average size of 3.16 nm and the rich of functional groups. It gives an ultra strong fluorescence emission with the fluorescent quantum yield of 90.91% and sensitive and selective optical response towards Hg2+. The fluorescence intensity linearly decreases with the increase of Hg2+ in the range of 1.0 × 10−10–2 × 10−4 M with the detection limit of 4.6 × 10−11 M (S/N = 3). No species tested interfere with detection of Hg2+. The fluorescence quenched by Hg2+ can be well recovered by glutathione. The fluorescence intensity linearly increases with the increase of glutathione in the range of 5 × 10−8–2.5 × 10−6 M with the detection limit of 1.7 × 10−8 M (S/N = 3). The PEHA-GQD-DPA as a fluorescence probe has been successfully applied in determination of Hg2+ in natural water and glutathione in human serum and SW480 cell imaging.

The paper reports synthesis of pentaethylenehexamine and d-penicillamine co-functionalized graphene quantum dots. The unique structure achieves to excellent optical behaviors of graphene quantum dots. The fluorescence probe has been successfully used for sequential detection of Hg2+, glutathione and bioimaging.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury(II) ion (Hg2+) is one of the most ubiquitous heavy metal pollutant that is serious threat to human health, because of its highly reactive nature that binds thiol groups in some proteins and enzymes, and a lesser degree to hydroxyl, carboxyl and phosphoryl groups [1]. Hg2+contamination may come from natural sources and human activities, including oceanic and volcanic emissions, solid waste incineration and combustion of fossil fuels [2]. The annual release amount of Hg2+ was about 4400–7500 metric tons estimated by the United Nations Environment Programme [3]. Hg2+ easily passes through skin, respiratory and gastrointestinal tissues and leads to DNA damage, mitosis impairment and permanent damage to the central nervous system [4]. Even at a low concentration, Hg2+ can enter food chain and constitute a serious threat to human health and natural environment [5]. Developing effective method for detection of Hg2+ is highly desirable.

Mostly used methods for the detection of Hg2+ are inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry [6], atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy [7] and selective cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry [8]. These methods often require the use of expensive and sophisticated instrumentation or complicated sample preparation processes [9]. In the recent years, many modern technologies have also been developed for the detection of Hg2+, such as gold nanorods enhanced resonance Rayleigh scattering [10], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [11], colorimetric sensor [12], quartz crystal microbalance [13], near-infrared chemosensor [14], electrochemical sensors [15] and fluorescent methods [16]. The fluorescence analysis with high sensitivity, fast detection and non-sample destructing features has been proved to be an alternative method for detection of Hg2+ [17]. Metal nanoparticles [18], semiconductor quantum dots [19], carbon nanoparticles [20] and organic small molecules [21], g-C3N4 nanoparticles [22] and Schiff base [23] have been explored and synthesized for optical detection of Hg2+. However, these fluorescent materials suffer from involving toxic reagents, poor selectivity or tedious synthesis. Consequently, it is urgent to develop the eco-friendly and low-cost Hg2+ fluorescent probes with a lower detection limit and higher selectivity.

Graphene quantum dot (GQD) is one zero-dimensional graphitic nanocrystals with the size of only several nanometers, strong quantum confinement and edge effects [24,25]. Due to outstanding fluorescent properties with tunable excitations and emissions, low toxicity and high chemical stability, GQDs have been widely applied in optical detection [26,27], biological imaging [28], electrochemical sensor [29], nanomaterial synthesis [30] and lithium ion batteries [31]. To date, considerable efforts have been devoted to synthesis of GQDs. One method is to make GQDs by cutting large graphene sheets via the acidic oxidation [32]. The method is time-consuming and the resulted GQDs lack of the rich of functional groups and special structures. Another method is to make GQDs by thermal pyrolysis of organic acids. The method is simple and effective and it can achieve to the formation of graphene sheets and their functionalization in one-step reaction. More importantly, the method is easy to introduce functional groups and structures [33]. The studies have demonstrated that the introduction of functional groups may alter the electrical characteristics, leading to unusual properties and related applications of GQDs [34].

In the study, we report a facile synthesis of pentaethylenehexamine (PEHA) and penicillamine (DPA) co-functionalized graphene quantum dots, which is termed as PEHA-GQD-DPA. The results show that the use of two-step synthesis strategy effectively circumvents producing impurities caused by the reactions between citric acid, PEHA and DPA. The introduction of PEHA and DPA greatly improves the fluorescence emission of GQD. The unique structure of PEHA-GQD-DPA achieves to a sensitive and selective fluorescence response towards Hg2+. It has been successfully applied in optical determination of Hg2+ and glutathione and cell bioimaging.

Citric acid, pentaethylenehexamine, d-penicillamine, glutathione and mercury chloride (HgCl2) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Mainland, China). Other reagents were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). All chemicals used were of analytical grade. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 5.5, Na2HPO4-KH2PO4-NaCl-KCl, 0.01 M) was prepared in the laboratory. Ultra pure water (18.2 MΩ cm) purified from Milli-Q purification system was used throughout experiment.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were conducted on a JEOL 2010 FEG microscope at 200 keV. The samples were prepared by dispensing a small amount of dry powder in the water. Then, one drop of the suspension was dropped on 300 mesh copper TEM grids covered with thin amorphous carbon films. Infrared spectra (IR) were recorded on a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) image was obtained on a Multimode 8 force microscope (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out on PHI 5700 ESCA spectrometer using Al HR radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV). Fluorescence spectrum and intensity were recorded on Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, Japan). pH was measured on PHS-3D pH meter (Shanghai Precision Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., China). UV–Visible absorption spectrum was measured on Shimadzu UV-2550 UV/visible spectrophotometer.

The PEHA-GQD-DPA was synthesized by one two-step continuous thermal pyrolysis. In a typical procedure, citric acid (4.2 g) was dissolved in ultra pure water (200 mL). Slowly dropped pentaethylenehexamine (1.86 g) into the solution under stirring. The mixed solution was transferred into the autoclave pressure vessel of 250 mL. Followed by heating at 200 °C for 1.5 h and cooling to room temperature. After added d-penicillamine (1.2 g) into the above solution under stirring, it was heated at 200 °C for 2 h and cooled to room temperature. The obtained PEHA-GQD-DPA sample was dissolved in ultra pure water to form a transparent PEHA-GQD-DPA solution (40 mg·mL−1). The solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag with a 3 kDa cut-off molecular weight with change of water every 6 h before the precipitate occurred in the bag. To obtain a solid PEHA-GQD-DPA sample, the solution in the bag was dried by free-drying. In addition, three control samples, GQD, PEHA-GQD and DPA-GQD, were also made by thermal pyrolysis of citric acid, the mixture of citric acid and pentaethylenehexamine, and the mixture of citric acid and d-penicillamine, respectively.

Quantum yield measurement was carried out by dissolving quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (used as a reference). The PEHA-GQD-DPA dispersion was used as such. The absorbance of respective sample was measured in the spectrophotometer. Quantum yield was calculated from following Eq. (1) [35]:
(1)
Q =
Q R
m s
m r
n s 2
n r 2
where, Q is the quantum yield of PEHA-GQD-DPA, QR is the quantum yield of quinine sulphate, ms is slope of the plot integrated fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance of the PEHA-GQD-DPA, mr is the slope of the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance of reference quinine sulphate, ns and nr are the refractive indices of the sample and reference, respectively, in the distilled water, which are assumed to be equal to that of water (1.33). The emission spectra for the sample were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 356 nm, keeping slit width at 2 nm.

The PEHA-GQD-DPA dispersion (600 μL, 20 μg·mL−1) was mixed with the PBS (3 mL, pH 5.5). Then, added the standard solution of Hg2+ with a known concentration. The mixed solution was equilibrated for 5 min and subsequently subjected to fluorescence measurement on fluorescence spectrophotometer with excitation wavelength of 360 nm. For the each of Hg2+detections, the fluorescence measurement was repeated thrice, and average fluorescence signal was obtained.

The Hg-PEHA-GQD-DPA dispersion was prepared by using the same procedure described in Section 2.5. The formed complex dispersion (1 mL) was mixed with the glutathione standard solution. After equilibrated for 5 min, the fluorescence spectrum was measured on fluorescence spectrophotometer with excitation wavelength of 360 nm. For the each of glutathione detections, the fluorescence measurement was repeated thrice, and average fluorescence signal was obtained.

SW480 cells were cultured in the DMEM containing 10% FBS and 1% penicillin-streptomycin at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 and 95% air. Before the imaging experiment, the cells were seeded in 24-well plates for 24 h. The cells were incubated with the PEHA-GQD-DPA (20 μg·mL−1) at 37 °C under 5% CO2 for 6 h, washed 3 times to remove the remaining probe and bathed in the DMEM containing no FBS prior to imaging. Then, the standard Hg2+ solution was added in the growth medium for 15 min at 37 °C, washed 3 times with the PBS. The cells were imaged under an inverted fluorescence microscopy. After that, the cells were washed 3 times using the PBS of pH 7. The fluorescence images were characterized with an excitation wavelength of 450 nm.

One two-step thermal pyrolysis was designed to synthesis of PEHA-GQD-DPA (shown in Fig. 1 ). The mixture of citric acid and PEHA was pyrolyzed into PEHA-GQD and then functionalized with DPA to form PEHA-GQD-DPA. Here, citric acid was used for forming hexagonal sp2-bonded carbon networks to make graphene sheets [36]. PEHA and DPA were used for introducing functional groups into the edge of graphene sheets. As PEHA and DPA contain sulfydryl, amino, imino, methyl, carboxyl and special structures, their introduction may result in unusual properties by altering the electrical characteristics. However, both PEHA and DPA are of highly chemical reactivity. The reactions between the same or different molecules in the system can produce many carbon quantum dots if the classical one-step hydrothermal method was adopted. However, the above problem can be effectively circumvented in our synthesis scheme. In the first step reaction, citric acid was fully changed into graphene sheets. Meanwhile, PEHA was covalently linked at the edges of graphene sheets. Due to their high reactivity, free citric acid and PEHA molecules in the system will be completely consumed and finally form PEHA-GQD. Only carboxyl group at the edge of graphene sheets can react with the sulfydryl or amino in DPA molecules to produce the PEHA-GQD-DPA in the second step reaction. Thus, our synthesis scheme can effectively circumvent producing impurities caused by the reactions between citric acid, PEHA and DPA.

To optimize the synthesis conditions, the ratios of PEHA and DPA were investigated. Fig. s1 presents the fluorescence emission spectra of PEHA-GQDs prepared by altering the molar ratio of PEHA/citric acid (PEHA/CA). The fluorescence intensity increases with the increase of PEHA if the ratio is lower than 0.4. The increased fluorescence is due to the introduction of PEHA. On the one hand, PEHA contains the rich of amino and imino groups, their electron donor effect will greatly improve the fluorescence emission. With increasing PEHA ratio number of electron-donating groups in the PEHA-GQD will rapidly increase. So, the use of a higher ratio of PEHA for synthesis of PEHA-GQD results in a stronger fluorescence emission. On the other hand, PEHA is easy to combine with citric acid via the covalent bond. The combination can improve the chemical activity of citric acid molecules because of the significant electron donor effect of PEHA. The high activity makes the dehydration between citric acid molecules has a higher reaction rate compared with single citric acid molecules. This leads to the formation of smaller and more graphene sheets. The conclusion was also confirmed by the result of ultraviolet spectrum analysis. Fig. s2 shows that the PEHA-GQD displays a relatively strong absorption spectrum with the maximum absorption peak at 350 nm. The absorbance increases with the increasing the ratio of PEHA, indicating the formation of more graphene sheets. However, continue to increase the ratio of PEHA will cause an obvious decrease on the absorbance and fluorescence if the ratio is bigger than 0.4. This is because the reaction between citric acid and PEHA molecules consumes a large number of carboxyl groups in the citric acid molecules. This reduces the ability to the formation of graphene sheets via the pyrolysis of citric acid and even results in the failure in formation of graphene sheets. To obtain a relatively strong fluorescence emission, the ratio of 0.4 was employed for synthesis of PEHA-GQD.

The absorption spectra and fluorescence emissions of PEHA-GQD-DPAs fabricated by using different ratio of DPA were shown in Fig. s3 and Fig. s4, respectively. Compared with the PEHA-GQD, the PEHA-GQD-DPA gives a similar absorption spectrum. The result verifies that the introduction of DPA does not affect on the size of graphene sheets and their particle size distribution. However, the PEHA-GQD-DPA has a stronger fluorescence emission. This is because DPA contains the rich of sulfydryl, amino and methyl groups. Their significant electron donor effect improves the fluorescence emission and leads to an increased fluorescence intensity. The use of a high ratio DPA will link more electron donor groups at the edge of graphene sheets, thus the absorbance and fluorescence of PEHA-GQD-DPA increases with the increase of DPA. However, the fluorescence intensity slightly decreases with the increase of DPA if the ratio of DPA is more than 0.4. This is due to the reduce in the water-solubility of graphene sheets with more DPAs at the edge. The experiment proved that obvious precipitation appears in the reaction system when the DPA ratio is more than 0.4. The precipitation decreases the concentration of PEHA-GQD-DPA in the aqueous solution, thus leading to a reduced fluorescence intensity. To obtain high fluorescence emission, the DPA/CA of 0.4 was used for synthesis of PEHA-GQD-DPA.

The as-prepared PEHA-GQD-DPA was characterized by TEM, IR spectrum, AFM and XPS technologies and the results were shown in Fig. 2 . It can be seen from Fig. 2 that the PEHA-GQD-DPA is composed of quasi-zero-dimensional graphene sheets. These sheets have homogeneous morphology without obvious agglomeration. Their size is in the range from 1 nm to 6 nm with the average size of 3.16 nm. The size is obviously smaller than that of previously reported GQDs [37]. This is mainly attributed to high chemical activity of PEHA. The high activity makes PEHA easy to combine with citric acid to form PEHA-GQD. The reaction consumes the carboxyl groups in the citric acid molecule. The reduce of carboxyl groups in the citric acid molecule limits the growth of graphene sheets via through the dehydration between citric acid molecules and subsequent carbonization, leading to the formation of smaller graphene sheets. Due to a stronger quantum confinement effect, the smaller graphene sheets may offer a higher fluorescence emission. There are many IR absorption bands and absorption peaks on the IR spectrum. The absorption bands at about 3500, 1698.89 and 1298.33 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH, CO and COH, indicating –COOH on the surface of PEHA-GQD-DPA. The characteristic absorption bands of –NH, –NCO and NHCO occurred at about 3400, 1650.12 and 1246.22 cm−1. The band between 3000 cm−1 and 2800 cm−1 is stretching vibration IR absorption of the saturated –CH bond. The AFM analysis reveals that the height of graphene sheets are less than 0.6 nm, indicating that the PEHA-GQD-DPA has two layers of graphene oxide-like structure.

Fig. s5 presents XPS spectrum of the PEHA-GQD-DPA. The total XPS spectrum offers four XPS peaks at 163, 284, 400 and 531 eV. These peaks are assigned to sulfur, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements. This verifies that the PEHA-GQD-DPA is composed of sulfur, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements. The S2p XPS spectrum contains two XPS peaks at 163.2 and 165.5 eV. The peaks correspond to C-Ssp3/2 and C-Ssp1/2, indicating the existence of CS bond. C1s XPS spectrum offers three XPS peaks at 284.2, 285.6 and 288.4 eV. The peaks are assigned to CC, COH and CO species. The N1s XPS spectrum includes three XPS peaks at 398.4, 399.5 and 401 eV. The peak at 398.4 eV is correspond to pyridinic N, the peak at 399.5 eV is correspond to amine N, amide N or pyrrolic N, and the peak at 401 eV is correspond to graphitic N. From Fig. s5D, it can be seen that the peak intensities of amine N is higher than that of pyridinic N and graphitic N. The result demonstrates that amine N is dominant in the PEHA-GQD-DPA.

Fig. 3 presents the fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of PEHA-GQD-DPA. On the fluorescence excitation spectrum there is a wide and strong absorption peak between 300 nm and 430 nm. As the maximum absorption wavelength of 360 nm is much bigger than that of small molecule compounds, the above result also proves the formation of the graphene sheets with a large π-electron conjugate system during the synthesis. On the fluorescence emission there is a symmetrical spectrum with maximum emission wavelength of 450 nm. The wavelength is bigger than that of the GQDs prepared by thermal hydrolysis of citric acid (422 nm). This is due to the introduction of PEHA and DPA. Their significant electron donor effect will greatly increase the electron density of graphene sheets. This leads to a red-shift of fluorescence spectrum of PEHA-GQD-DPA.

To compare with the fluorescence emission, the fluorescence spectra of PEHA-GQD-DPA, PEHA-GQD and DPA-GQD were listed in Fig. s6. It can be seen from Fig. s6 that The PEHA-GQD-DPA offers the strongest fluorescence emission among three GQDs. Further, the fluorescence quantum yield of PEHA-GQD-DPA was measured. The result shows that fluorescence quantum yield is up to 90.91%. The value is much higher than that of previously reported GQDs in Table s1. To evaluate the photo-stability, the quartz cuvette filled with the PEHA-GQD-DPA was radiated under a Xenon lamp (300 W). The experiment shows that the fluorescence intensity keeps unchanged within the irradiating time of 90 min, verifying an excellent photo-stability.

The fluorescence spectra of PEHA-GQD-DPA was investigated in the PBS of pH 5.5 in the absence and the presence of 1 × 10−4 M Hg2+. Fig. s7A shows that the introduction of Hg2+ causes obvious fluorescence quenching. The PEHA-GQD-DPA contains the rich of amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups that are easy to combine with Hg2+ via the coordination bonds, thus the above fluorescence quenching is due to the formation of Hg-PEHA-GQD-DPA complex. However, the coordination may occur either within graphene sheet or between different graphene sheets. To understand the quenching mechanism, the resonant light scattering (RLS) behavior of PEHA-GQD-DPA was studied in the absence and the presence of Hg2+. Fig. s7B indicates that the RLS intensity obviously increases after added Hg2+. The result demonstrates that formation of the complex results in an increased particle size. Thus, we suggest that the coordination between Hg2+ and PEHA-GQD-DPA mainly occurs between different graphene sheets.

The relationship of the fluorescence response with pH values was presented at Fig. s8. The change in the peak fluorescence intensity (ΔF) rapidly increases with the increase of pH when the pH is <5.5. This verifies that the protonation of PEHA-GQD-DPA can be bad for forming Hg-PEHA-GQD-DPA. The ΔF value rapidly reduces with the increase of pH value. This proves that the dissociation of PEHA-GQD-DPA can be also bad for forming Hg-PEHA-GQD-DPA complex. To obtain high sensitivity and selectivity, the PBS of pH 5.5 was used as the reaction medium for fluorescent detection of Hg2+.

Fig. 4 presents the fluorescence spectra of PEHA-GQD-DPA in the presence of different Hg2+concentration in the PBS of pH 5.5. It can be seen from Fig. 4A that a higher concentration of Hg2+ will lead to a bigger degree of the fluorescence quenching. The peak fluorescence intensity (F) linearly decreases with the increase of Hg2+ concentration in the range from 1 × 10−10 to 2 × 10−4 M. The linear equation was: F = −83.867logC + 36.548, with a statistically significant correlation coefficient of 0.997 (R2), where F is the peak fluorescence intensity and C is the concentration of Hg2+ (M). The detection limit was 4.6 × 10−11 M (S/N = 3). Such sensitivity is better than that of reported optical sensors for Hg2+ in Table s2.

The interference study was carried by measurement peak fluorescence intensity of the PEHA-GQD-DPA before and after added inorganic ion and small molecule compound. The main metallic ions in natural water are Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+ and K+, and main nonmetallic ions are Cl−, CO3 2−, SO4 2− and C2O4 2−. The result indicates that the each ion of 1 × 10−2 M only brings the change on the peak fluorescence intensity of less than 2%. These ions can not react with PEHA-GQD-DPA to form stable complex in the PBS of pH 5.5. Thus, the existence of above ions can not interfere with optical detection of Hg2+. Except for the inorganic ions, some small molecular compounds may also exist in natural water samples, including ascorbic acid, urea, hydrogen peroxide, dopamine, threonine, glycine, alanine, lysine and proline that may come from plant corruption and microbial metabolism. The result shows that the each compound of 1 × 10−5 M only causes the change on the peak fluorescence intensity of less than 3.5%. Because the concentration of above ions and small molecule compounds tested are far more than their possible content, the proposed method can be directly applied to optical detect Hg2+ in natural water samples.

Main functional groups in the PEHA-GQD-DPA are amino, imino, hydroxyl and carboxyl. Most likely interference may come from transition metallic ions, because they are easy to coordinate with oxygen and nitrogen atoms to form stable metal complex. To further evaluate the selectivity, the effect of Cr3+, Fe3+, Co3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and Ag+ on the optical detection of 1 × 10−6 M Hg2+ was also researched. Fig. s9 indicates that the each transition metallic ion of 1 × 10−3 M only causes a small change in the peak fluorescence intensity of less than 5%. Such a high concentration of transition metallic ion does not interfere with the optical detection of Hg2+, indicating excellent selectivity. The selectivity is much better than that of previously reported graphene quantum dots for Hg2+ in Table s3. The excellent selectivity is attributed to the special configuration made up of graphene sheet and its functional groups on the edges.

The PEHA-GQD-DPA was used as optical sensor for fluorescence determination of Hg2+ in natural water samples. All samples were collected from near Wuxi city. The calibration curve was employed for calculating Hg2+ concentration in the original water sample. The recovery experiments were performed by measuring the fluorescence response to the sample before and after added a known concentration of standard Hg2+ solution. The results were listed in Table 1 . The recovery of 95.4–104.2% was obtained, verifying that the proposed method has high accuracy.

The fluorescence response of Hg-PEHA-GQD-DPA complex towards S2O3 2−, S2−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, glutathione, cysteine, methionine and lysine were studied. It was found that only glutathione causes an obvious increase in the peak fluorescence intensity of Hg-PEHA-GQD-DPA complex among these species. The cysteine brings the change on the fluorescence intensity of less than 30%. The interference of S2O3 2−, S2−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, methionine and lysine was very small. The fact confirms that the Hg-PEHA-GQD-DPA has good selectivity and sensitivity for optical detection of glutathione. Fig. 5 presents the fluorescence spectrum of Hg-PEHA-GQD-DPA in the presence of different concentration glutathione. It can be seen that the peak fluorescence intensity linearly increases with the increase of glutathione in the range from 5 × 10−8 to 2.5 × 10−6 M with the detection limit of 1.7 × 10−8 M (S/N = 3). The linear fitted equation is F = 64.648C + 117.65, with a correlation coefficient of 0.992.

The Hg-PEHA-GQD-DPA complex was used as a fluorescence probe for fluorescence detection of glutathione in human serum. Table 2 shows that the result obtained by the proposed method is a very good agreement between the results obtained by the proposed method and those obtained by HPLC [38]. The prospective results by our method possessed the feasibility and reliability for real clinical sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sequential Recognition of Hg2+ and Glutathione in Living Cells
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent imaging analysis was used to prove the value of PEHA-GQD-DPA in bioimaging intracellular Hg2+ and glutathione in SW480 cell (shown in Fig. 6 ). These cells were incubated with the PEHA-GQD-DPA solution in the DMEM medium for 30 min at 37 °C and then washed with the PBS of pH 7 to remove excess PEHA-GQD-DPA. As determined by fluorescent imaging microscopy, the staining cells treated with PEHA-GQD-DPA for 30 min gives a green intracellular fluorescence. After the treatment with the Hg2+ solution, the fluorescence from the intracellular region will disappear. However, the fluorescence can be well recovered by the treatment with glutathione for 15 min. The above results have demonstrated that the PEHA-GQD-DPA can be used for sequential detection of Hg2+ and glutathione in living cells.

The study has demonstrated a facile synthesis of pentaethylenehexamine and d-penicillamine co-functionalized graphene quantum dots via one two-step hydrothermal method. The introduction of pentaethylenehexamine and d-penicillamine greatly improves the fluorescence emission of graphene quantum dots. The characteristic makes the graphene quantum dots can be widely used as fluorescence probe for various bioimaging. The small graphene sheets and their edge groups forms a unique structure. The structure achieves to a highly sensitive and selectively fluorescence response two Hg2+. The study also provides an approach for design and synthesis of graphene quantum dots with excellent optical and electrical properties for various detections, sensors and bioimagings.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115), Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision Inspection Institute (KJ(Y) 2017014) and MOE & SAFEA for the 111 Project (B13025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.05.118.
